home.title: One kit — a ready development process for any AI agent
home.lead: AI Kit adds a development workflow on top of your AI agent. The same set of commands works in Claude Code, Cursor, OpenCode, Aider and Qwen Code: new features, bug fixes, review checkpoints, isolated sessions for messy fixes, autonomous runs.

stats.label: from empty repo to a working dev process
the workflow runs in each of them
your code stays on your machine

what.title: What you get with AI Kit
what.lead: You get a development workflow that doesn't depend on which AI tool you happen to use today. A new hire runs one command and joins the same workflow as everyone else. If the team moves from Cursor to Claude Code, nothing about the workflow changes. Checkpoints stop the agent before risky steps; you can turn them off when you want it to run unattended.

cards.title: .aikit/manifest.yaml
cards.body: A single YAML for the whole project. Describes the stack, modules, rules, agent definitions, and which tools to use.

cards.title: kit-setup CLI
cards.body: A binary that reads the manifest and lays out configs. Runs locally, no account, no network after install.

cards.title: Profiles
cards.body: Reusable manifest fragments for common stacks and frameworks: kotlin-gradle, typescript-pnpm, nextjs, security-baseline, and more.

cards.title: Render targets
cards.body: The list of AI tools you want configs rendered for. Five today; new ones come from files in kit-setup/templates/.

cards.title: Agents
cards.body: Role definitions (review, security, refactor, etc.) become real Claude Code subagents or OpenCode assistants.

cards.title: Constitution
cards.body: Sections of project rules. They become the body of CLAUDE.md and the .mdc files for Cursor.

what.summary: One workflow, set up once, runs on every AI agent your team uses.

how.title: From an empty repo to ready development workflows
how.lead: Everything runs locally. No accounts, no remote services, no signup.

steps.title: Open an AI agent in your project
steps.body: Either Claude Code or OpenCode — the prompt assumes one of those. Run it inside your repo's directory. If you don't have either, install one first.

steps.title: Paste the prompt into chat
steps.body: There's one line on the home page that says, in effect, «fetch the instructions at this URL and follow them». From there the agent takes over.

steps.title: Agent drafts the manifest and verifies it
steps.body: It looks at the project — languages, frameworks, how the modules are arranged — then writes .aikit/manifest.yaml and runs kit-setup verify. If verify finds an error, the agent fixes it and re-runs. That loop is part of the normal flow, not a sign of trouble.

steps.title: Agent renders the configs
steps.body: kit-setup generate writes the per-tool files: CLAUDE.md and .claude/agents/* for Claude Code, .cursor/rules/* for Cursor, AGENTS.md and opencode.json for OpenCode, and so on. You'll see the list of paths it created.

scenarios.title: Where it pays off most
scenarios.lead: Three situations where doing this by hand eats more time than it looks like at the start.

items.title: Starting a fresh Next.js project
items.body: Cursor opens on an empty project and suggests something off, because it knows nothing specific about this repo. With AI Kit you paste the prompt. The agent reads package.json, recognises Next.js + TS + pnpm, pulls in profiles typescript-pnpm, nextjs, security-baseline. A minute later .cursor/rules/*.mdc are in the repo — and Cursor stops suggesting var or require.

items.title: Migrating the team from Cursor to Claude Code
items.body: Seven files under .cursor/rules. The team is switching to Claude Code, which wants CLAUDE.md, .claude/agents/*, slash commands, settings.json. Instead of porting it all by hand into the new formats — fill out .aikit/manifest.yaml once. Generate renders for both Cursor (during the transition) and Claude Code.

items.title: Keeping the agent out of legacy
items.body: A folder slated for rewrite, but off-limits for now. Add legacy/ to forbidden_patterns — AI Kit bakes the rule into every generated config. The same restriction shows up in Cursor, Claude Code, and Aider, instead of you copying the same sentence into three different files.

usecases.title: What it looks like in practice
usecases.lead: A few typical stories with step-by-step actions. The first three are open — the rest are collapsed; click to expand.

items.title: Bootstrapping a Next.js project
steps: Created the repo and ran create-next-app.
Opened Claude Code or OpenCode in the same directory.
Pasted the prompt. The agent reads package.json, recognises Next.js + TypeScript + pnpm, suggests profiles typescript-pnpm + nextjs + security-baseline.
About a minute later there's .aikit/manifest.yaml plus a set of .cursor/rules/*.mdc and CLAUDE.md (depending on which render_targets you kept).
items.watch: Watch for: the first verify sometimes points out a missing module or an ambiguous rule. The agent fixes it and re-runs verify. The «verify → edit → verify» loop is part of the normal flow, not a failure.

items.title: Migrating the team from Cursor to Claude Code
steps: Open .cursor/rules — seven files have piled up over the past six months.
Run the prompt. The agent reads the existing rules and folds them into .aikit/manifest.yaml: constitution, agent definitions, which models to use.
In render_targets keep both: claude-code and cursor. The team migrates gradually — both tools work off the same source of truth.
When the migration is complete, drop cursor from render_targets and re-run generate. The old .cursor/* files remain on disk but stop receiving updates.
items.watch: Common pitfall: expecting the old .cursor/rules/*.mdc to disappear after you remove the target. AI Kit doesn't delete files outside the current render — clean up .cursor/ yourself.

items.title: Hiding legacy from the agent
steps: Open .aikit/manifest.yaml.
Add legacy/ under forbidden_patterns.
Run kit-setup generate.
CLAUDE.md, the .mdc files, and CONVENTIONS.md now contain the rule to leave legacy/ alone.
items.watch: Path is relative to the project root; for a directory, end it with a slash. Want to forbid more — add another line, the format is free.

items.title: One project, different models for different tasks
steps: Each agent in the manifest has a model field. You can set different models per role: review-agent on a strong model, lint-agent on a fast cheap one.
If a particular AI tool doesn't support a model, kit-setup verify reports it — it knows which models are available where.
When a model isn't valid for a target, the prompt-driven agent will either swap it out or drop the target from render_targets.
items.watch: This is a hand-tuning step. Easier to do after the AI agent has put together a baseline manifest — let the prompt do the draft, then assign models per agent.

items.title: Adding a fourth tool six months later
steps: Open .aikit/manifest.yaml.
Add the new target to render_targets — say, aider.
Run kit-setup verify. If two targets want the same file, verify rejects the combo with a clear message.
Resolve it — for example, drop opencode and keep qwen-code, since they both write AGENTS.md.
Run generate.
items.watch: The known collision is opencode and qwen-code on AGENTS.md. Verify catches it. All other targets coexist fine.

items.title: Freelancer with ten clients
steps: Each client has their own stack. For a new one, start the manifest from a profile: kotlin-gradle, typescript-pnpm, python-poetry, make-generic.
Layer the client's specifics on top: modules, conventions, forbidden paths.
Generate.
items.watch: Profiles cover the typical bits; everything specific is written on top. You don't have to keep someone else's rules in your head — the manifest in the client's repo always tells you.

flows.title: Day-to-day with the kit
flows.lead: How the slash commands compose into the developer's daily loop. The first one runs once at project start; the rest repeat per feature or fix.

items.title: 1. Set up the kit (once per project)
steps: Paste the setup prompt — the agent reads the project, drafts .aikit/manifest.yaml.
kit-setup verify .aikit/manifest.yaml — fix any errors the JSON reports.
kit-setup generate .aikit/manifest.yaml — render per-tool configs (CLAUDE.md, .claude/agents/*, .cursor/rules/*, AGENTS.md, etc., depending on render_targets).
items.watch: Re-run the setup prompt when the stack changes (new framework, new module, new model). Generate is idempotent — overwrites cleanly.

items.title: 2. Develop a new feature
steps: /kit-prepare <rough idea> — intake assistant turns it into a clean prompt and recommends the next /kit-* command.
/kit-new-feature <description> — risk-classified (trivial / standard / critical), drives the 6-stage pipeline.
@Architect drafts spec.md + plan.md → CONFIRM gate awaits /kit-approve.
EXECUTE step-by-step with per-step commits and ground-truth artefacts.
At each 5.6 CHECKPOINT — review the runbook + Context Snapshot Block; /kit-approve to advance, /kit-defect to re-open in-session, or use the defect-isolation flow below.
After the last step: RECONCILE → TRACE → DoD gate → DIFF-REVIEW → CLOSE.
items.watch: Sleep mode (/kit-sleep) collapses CONFIRM and 5.6 user-forks for autonomous runs — use it when you're away from the screen and trust the lane.

items.title: 3. Fix a known bug
steps: /kit-fix — scans the active feature's test-cases.md for FAIL / PEND rows, asks which ones to fix.
/kit-fix TC-42 — direct mode for one test case ID.
/kit-fix Save button stays disabled — appends a new TC and runs the BUG pipeline (TRIAGE → DEBUG → FIX → RE-VERIFY → REPORT).
items.watch: Source of truth is vault/specs/features/<module>/<feature>/test-cases.md. Edit it directly to mark bugs as FAIL — /kit-fix picks them up next run.

items.title: 4. Defect-isolation flow (keeps the main session's context clean)
steps: Original session at 5.6 emits a Context Snapshot Block (copyable) alongside the verification request.
Open a NEW session in the same project directory.
/kit-fix <defect description> Контекст: <paste Snapshot Block> — the new session fixes the defect with only that context, never reading .planning/CURRENT.md.
When verified, /kit-approve in the new session — the agent emits a Decision Context Block and stops.
Return to the original session: /kit-fixed <paste Decision Block> — state syncs (step_commits[N] updated, TC marked PASS), 5.6 fork re-presents.
/kit-approve in the original session to advance.
items.watch: Use this for non-trivial fixes where the back-and-forth would bloat the main pipeline session. For one-line typos, /kit-defect in-session is faster.

commands.title: CLI commands
commands.lead: If you'd rather skip the AI agent, all of these run by hand. Download the binary from the Releases page.

items.body: Validates the manifest. Emits {"valid": bool, "errors": [...]}. Used in the «edit → check» loop.
Renders the configs. Emits {"ok": bool, "generated": [...]}. Overwrites its own files on every run.

Lists the profiles, agents, targets, and commands bundled in this binary, plus `enums` with allowed values for validator-enforced fields (provider_auth, model_tier, cost_hint, knowledge_store_kind). Default is JSON; --format human prints a readable tree.
Prints the binary version and exits.

exit.body: Manifest invalid (verify) or refused by validation (generate).
Usage error, manifest load failure, parse error, or runtime I/O failure.

profiles.title: What it's assembled from
profiles.lead: You can write the manifest from scratch, but most of the time it's easier to plug in ready profiles. A profile is a manifest fragment someone else already wrote. Profiles are grouped along three axes.

axes.name: language — exactly one
axes.body: Your project's main language and build tool. The profile drops in build, test, and lint commands plus typical forbidden paths.

axes.name: framework — any number
axes.body: By framework or platform. Adds UI hints and characteristic rules.

axes.name: capability — any number
axes.body: Cross-stack policies. Toggle them as needed.

targets.title: What lands in the project per tool
targets.lead: The list of render targets and what each drops into the repo. Add to render_targets — config gets rendered; remove it — that config stops updating.

items.body: Top-level guidance, subagent definitions, slash commands, permissions and MCP servers. Constitution flows into CLAUDE.md.
One .mdc file per constitution section, alwaysApply: true. Plus an MCP config. Cursor doesn't have slash commands, so those aren't rendered.
AGENTS.md as the main guidance, assistant definitions, slash commands, provider/MCP block. Don't enable simultaneously with qwen-code — they both write AGENTS.md.
CONVENTIONS.md, which Aider auto-loads, plus a global config with model binding. Prompts ship as separate files.
Mirror of the opencode layout but in its own folder. settings.json contains the modelProviders block for Qwen.

example.title: One manifest, what comes out of it
example.lead: A minimal working .aikit/manifest.yaml — about ten lines. From it AI Kit renders CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules/*.mdc, AGENTS.md, and the rest.
example.generatedLabel: CLAUDE.md — fragment of what comes out
example.note: From those ten lines AI Kit produces about 9–15 files for the chosen render targets. You don't need to remember the full list — every generate prints it.

support.title: What to do when AI Kit gets in the way
support.lead: The utility doesn't put down roots. All artifacts live inside the project; delete and forget.

items.title: Updating
items.body: Download the new binary from the Releases page or build from git. Run kit-setup generate again — configs update against the latest templates.

items.title: Extending
items.body: Adding a new profile or adapter for a new tool means adding a file under kit-setup/templates/. After a rebuild the binary knows the new target. Pull requests welcome.

items.title: Removing
items.body: Delete .aikit/, the generated CLAUDE.md, .cursor/, .opencode/, and similar. The binary is a regular executable — delete it like any file. Nothing is registered system-wide.

faq.title: Frequently asked
faq.lead: What newcomers ask, and what AI Kit does about it.

items.q: Every AI agent works its own way, and there's no shared process across the team.
items.a: In all five tools the workflow is the same: /kit-prepare, /kit-new-feature, /kit-fix, the same checkpoints, the same evidence checks. A developer can switch between Claude Code and Cursor mid-week without changing how they work.

items.q: Cursor keeps poking around in the legacy folder we shouldn't touch yet.
items.a: Add legacy/ to forbidden_patterns in the manifest. The rule lands in every generated config — Cursor, Claude Code, Aider all stay out of that folder. No need to copy the same sentence into three different files.

items.q: The team wants shared rules, but everyone configures Cursor their own way.
items.a: The manifest lives in the repo and travels with the project. A new teammate runs git pull and kit-setup generate — their AI works by the same rules as everyone else's. The config becomes part of the codebase, not a personal setup.

items.q: I don't want to create another account just for a utility.
items.a: There isn't one. AI Kit is a single binary: reads YAML from disk, writes files next to it. Network is only needed once, when you download it from the Releases page.

items.q: I'm worried generate will break what I've already tweaked by hand.
items.a: Generate only overwrites files it created itself: CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules/*, AGENTS.md and the like. Your project's own source files are untouched. But edits made directly in the generated files will be lost on the next run — move those edits into the manifest or its templates.

items.q: I don't like YAML and don't want to learn it.
items.a: Then don't. Open Claude Code or OpenCode in your project, paste the prompt from the home page — the agent surveys the project, drafts the manifest, and runs kit-setup itself. Your part is one paste in chat.

items.q: My stack isn't a standard one.
items.a: Language and framework fields are free strings — write whatever fits. If there's a profile that matches, add one line to stack.profiles. If not, describe the specifics yourself; the manifest extends freely without a strict schema for values.

Nothing. AI Kit is open source under Apache 2.0. No subscriptions, no tiers. The AI assistants themselves (Cursor, Claude Code and so on) are billed separately by their vendors — AI Kit doesn't change that.

items.q: Do I need to know how to code?
items.a: To use it — no. If you can run an AI agent in chat (Claude Code, OpenCode), paste a prompt, and read the result, you're set.

The binary doesn't phone home. It reads the manifest from disk and writes files alongside. The prompt you paste into your AI agent fetches one markdown file from GitHub — that's the only network call. Your code and manifest stay on your machine; they only reach the cloud through the AI assistant you're already using.
Generate only overwrites its own files. If the result is off — fix the manifest and re-run. Worst case, delete .aikit/ and the generated configs and the repo is back to where it started.

items.q: Does the kit overwrite existing files?
items.a: Yes, unconditionally — but only the ones it creates itself (CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules/*, AGENTS.md, etc.). Your own sources are untouched. If you've added something to a generated CLAUDE.md by hand, it'll be lost next run; move edits into the manifest.

items.q: Can I use it in commercial work?
items.a: Yes. Apache 2.0 allows it. Generated files belong to you — do whatever you need with them.

items.q: Which render targets should I pick?
items.a: Only the ones you actually use. Multiple at once is fine. The single restriction: opencode + qwen-code together collide on AGENTS.md and verify will reject the combo — pick one.
Only when downloading the binary. After that, no. Manifest is read from disk, files are written next to it; nothing is sent outward.

items.q: What if my language isn't in the list?
items.a: Put it under stack.languages — the field is free-form, the value flows into prompts via shared snippets. There won't be a ready profile for it, but that doesn't block anything.

items.q: I want to add my own tool or profile.
items.a: Tool adapters and profiles live in kit-setup/templates/. Add a file, rebuild the binary, and it knows the new target. Pull requests welcome in the repo.
Open an issue on the GitHub repository — link in the footer.

cta.title: Want to try it?
cta.body: Open Claude Code or OpenCode in your project and paste this prompt. About a minute later you'll see a list of files it created.

scenariosBanner.title: How teams actually use AI Kit
scenariosBanner.body: Six worked examples covering common situations: project bootstrap, migrating a team between AI tools, locking the agent out of legacy folders, picking different models per role, adding a new tool months later, and freelance work across multiple stacks.
